perturbation.original_text: 3.5.2 Payments that are not received within thirty (30) days after their due date will bear interest at the rate of twelve percent (12.0%) per annum compounded monthly from the due date until such payment is received.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.5.2 Payments that are not received within thirty (30) days after their due date will bear interest at the rate of eighteen percent (18.0%) per annum simple interest from the due date until such payment is received.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the interest rate to 18% simple interest may exceed the legal maximum allowed under Maryland usury laws for certain types of transactions, depending on the specific circumstances and nature of the agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: Maryland law sets limits on interest rates for certain types of loans and transactions. While the specific limits vary depending on the nature of the transaction (e.g., consumer loans, commercial loans), an interest rate of 18% simple interest could potentially exceed the maximum rate allowed, especially if the agreement is considered a loan or forbearance under Maryland law. This modification contradicts the state's usury laws if the rate is deemed excessive for the specific transaction type.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Message - Maryland Code and Court Rules This document is not available on Westlaw.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the interest rate to 18% simple interest, which the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation suggest could violate Maryland usury laws, especially if the agreement is considered a loan or forbearance. The original text had an interest rate of 12% compounded monthly, which is less likely to violate usury laws. Since both law snippets are unavailable, the contradiction is determined based on the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation, which state that 18% simple interest could potentially exceed the maximum rate allowed under Maryland law for specific transactions.

perturbation.original_text: 7.2 All actions, cases, suits and proceedings in connection with this Agreement shall be brought in Maryland. All persons affected by this Agreement specifically consent to the personal jurisdiction of and venue in said courts. No action, case, suit or proceeding, regardless of form, arising out of or related to this Agreement, may be brought by either party more than one (1) year after the cause of action has arisen, or in the case of nonpayment, more than two (2) years from the date of the last payment. ALL ACTIONS, CASES, SUITS AND PROCEEDINGS SHALL BE HEARD WITHOUT A JURY. ALL PERSONS AFFECTED BY THIS AGREEMENT SPECIFICALLY WAIVE ALL RIGHT TO A TRIAL BY JURY AND SPECIFICALLY CONSENT TO THE PERSONAL JURISDICTION OF AND VENUE IN SAID COURTS.
perturbation.changed_text: 7.2 All actions, cases, suits and proceedings in connection with this Agreement shall be brought in Maryland. All persons affected by this Agreement specifically consent to the personal jurisdiction of and venue in said courts. No action, case, suit or proceeding, regardless of form, arising out of or related to this Agreement, may be brought by either party more than six (6) months after the cause of action has arisen, or in the case of nonpayment, more than one (1) year from the date of the last payment. ALL ACTIONS, CASES, SUITS AND PROCEEDINGS SHALL BE HEARD WITHOUT A JURY. ALL PERSONS AFFECTED BY THIS AGREEMENT SPECIFICALLY WAIVE ALL RIGHT TO A TRIAL BY JURY AND SPECIFICALLY CONSENT TO THE PERSONAL JURISDICTION OF AND VENUE IN SAID COURTS.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the statute of limitations for bringing a claim to six months for most cases and one year for nonpayment is shorter than the standard limitations periods provided by Maryland law for contract claims.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Maryland Statute of Limitations for Contracts
perturbation.law_citation: Md. Code Ann., Cts. & Jud. Proc. § 5-101
perturbation.law_explanation: Maryland law provides a three-year statute of limitations for contract claims. By shortening the limitations period to six months or one year, the modified text contradicts the statutory provision, potentially making the contractual limitation unenforceable as against public policy or if deemed unreasonably short.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract text reduces the statute of limitations for contract claims to six months or one year, which is shorter than the three-year statute of limitations provided by Maryland law as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 2: "§5–101. A civil action at law shall be filed within three years from the date it accrues unless another provision of the Code provides a different period of time within which an action shall be commenced." This creates a legal contradiction because the contractual term directly conflicts with the statutory provision.

perturbation.original_text: 5. TERM This Agreement will remain in force for perpetuity or until and unless otherwise mutually agreed or amended in writing by both parties.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. TERM This Agreement will remain in force for ten (10) years, automatically renewing for successive one (1) year terms, unless terminated with ninety (90) days written notice by either party prior to the end of the then-current term. Such termination shall be at the terminating party's sole discretion.
perturbation.explanation: The original contract states the term is for perpetuity, while the modified version adds a termination clause at the terminating party's sole discretion.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract Law Principles - Illusory Promises/Good Faith
perturbation.law_explanation: While not a direct statutory contradiction, changing the term to allow termination at one party's 'sole discretion' without any requirement for good faith could render the contract's termination provision illusory under general contract law principles as articulated in the Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 77. An illusory promise is one that appears to be a promise but does not actually obligate the promisor to do anything. If a party can terminate for any reason whatsoever, including bad faith, the promise to continue the agreement is arguably not binding, potentially making the entire agreement unenforceable for lack of mutuality of obligation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: illusory promise | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you illusory promise An illusory promise is a promise that is unenforceable due to indefiniteness or lack of mutuality , where only one side is bound to perform. The promise is normally a statement that seems to be an offer or agreement , but is so vague, ambiguous or conditional that it does not actually obligate the promisor to do anything at all. An example of this would be an agreement between a seller and buyer which states that the seller "agrees to sell all of the ice cream he wants to" to the buyer. An important in contract law that illustrates this concept is Hamer v. Sidway, 27 N.E. 256 (N.Y. 1891 ). In this case, an uncle promised his nephew that he would pay him $5,000 if the nephew refrained from drinking, using tobacco, swearing, or gambling until the age of 21. The nephew fulfilled his side of the bargain, but the uncle refused to pay, arguing that the promise was merely a gift and not a binding contract. The court held that the promise was not illusory because the nephew had given up a legal right (to engage in those activities) in exchange for the promise of payment. [Last reviewed in March of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE business law contracts wex definitions business sectors legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The original contract provides for perpetuity, whereas the modified version creates a finite term that can be terminated at the terminating party's discretion. This introduces an element of an illusory promise, as stated in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines an illusory promise as 'a promise that is unenforceable due to indefiniteness or lack of mutuality, where only one side is bound to perform.' The modification introduces a termination clause at the sole discretion of one party, undermining the agreement's enforceability by potentially rendering the promise illusory, and contradicting the original agreement for perpetuity.
